prompt: An element is `<h1 id="title" class="heading">`. Both rules below apply. What color is the text?
choices: red — an `!important` declaration wins over a normal one regardless of specificity
blue — the ID selector `#title` has higher specificity, and specificity always wins
blue — it is written first, and the first matching rule wins
red — the class selector is more specific than the ID
explanation: The cascade resolves conflicts by origin/importance *first*, then specificity, then source order. `!important` bumps a declaration into a higher-priority band, so `red` beats `blue` even though `#title` (1,0,0) out-specifies `.heading` (0,1,0). Specificity only breaks ties *within* the same importance band — which is exactly why chasing bugs with `!important` is discouraged.

prompt: Two rules target the same `<a class="item">` inside the markup. Which color wins?
choices: blue — `#sidebar .item` is (1,1,0); one ID outranks the three classes of `.list .item.active` (0,3,0)
red — it has three classes, and more selectors always mean higher specificity
red — it comes later in the source, and later rules win
blue — element selectors count more than class selectors
explanation: Specificity is a tuple (IDs, classes, elements) compared left-to-right. `#sidebar .item` = (1,1,0); `.list .item.active` = (0,3,0). The ID column decides it immediately: 1 > 0, so blue wins and the class count never even gets compared. A single ID beats *any* number of classes.

prompt: With the default `content-box` sizing, what is the element's total rendered width (the space it occupies horizontally)?
explanation: With `content-box` (the default), `width` sizes only the content area; padding and border are added on top. Total = 300 + (20+20) padding + (10+10) border = 360px. Switching to `box-sizing: border-box` would instead make `width` include padding and border, keeping the box at 300px — which is why most resets set `border-box` globally.

prompt: You need to lay out a page with rows *and* columns that align to each other in both directions (a true 2D grid). Flexbox or Grid?
choices: CSS Grid — it is a 2D system that controls rows and columns together
Flexbox — it handles rows and columns simultaneously just as well
Flexbox — Grid can only create single columns
Neither; you must use floats for 2D layouts
explanation: Flexbox is a *1D* system: it lays items along a single axis (a row OR a column) and is ideal for toolbars, nav bars, and distributing space among items. Grid is *2D*: it defines rows and columns simultaneously so cells align in both directions — ideal for page-level layouts and card galleries. Rule of thumb: content-driven single-axis → flex; layout-driven two-axis → grid.

prompt: A container has `display: flex;` with the default `flex-direction: row`. Which declarations center its children both horizontally and vertically?
choices: `justify-content: center; align-items: center;` — main axis (horizontal) then cross axis (vertical)
`align-items: center; justify-content: center;` — `align-items` is horizontal, `justify-content` is vertical
`text-align: center; vertical-align: middle;`
`justify-items: center; align-content: center;`
explanation: In a flex row the *main axis* runs horizontally, so `justify-content` positions items horizontally; the *cross axis* runs vertically, so `align-items` positions them vertically. Center both with `justify-content: center; align-items: center;`. Note the mapping flips if you set `flex-direction: column` — then `justify-content` becomes vertical. (`flex: 1` on a child, meanwhile, tells it to grow and share leftover main-axis space.)

prompt: An element has `position: absolute; top: 0; left: 0;`. Relative to what is it positioned?
choices: The nearest ancestor that is itself positioned (`relative`/`absolute`/`fixed`/`sticky`); if none exists, the initial containing block (viewport)
Always the browser viewport, ignoring all ancestors
Its direct parent element, whatever that parent's `position` is
The `<body>` element, always
explanation: An absolutely-positioned element is offset from its *containing block* — the nearest ancestor whose `position` is anything other than `static`. That is why the common pattern is `position: relative` on the parent to "trap" an absolute child. Contrast: `fixed` is relative to the viewport, `sticky` acts like `relative` until a scroll threshold then sticks like `fixed` within its scroll container, and `static` (the default) ignores `top`/`left`.

choices: `opacity < 1` makes `.parent` a stacking context, so `.child`'s z-index only competes inside `.parent` — and `.parent` (z-index 1) sits below `.other` (z-index 2)
z-index values above 9999 are clamped by the browser to a lower number
`.child` needs `position: absolute` for z-index to take effect at all
Sibling elements always paint on top of nested elements regardless of z-index
explanation: `opacity` less than 1 (like `transform`, `filter`, `will-change`, etc.) creates a new stacking context on `.parent`. A child's z-index is only meaningful *within* its own stacking context; the whole `.parent` subtree is then stacked as a unit at `.parent`'s level. Since `.parent` (z-index 1) is below `.other` (z-index 2), no z-index on `.child` can lift it above `.other`. The fix is to raise `.parent`'s z-index or remove what created the context.

prompt: What is the most robust modern way to center a fixed-size box both horizontally and vertically inside its parent?
choices: Give the parent `display: grid; place-items: center;` (or flex with `justify-content`/`align-items: center`)
`margin: 0 auto;` on the box
`text-align: center;` on the parent
`vertical-align: middle;` on the box
explanation: `display: grid; place-items: center` (or `display: flex; justify-content: center; align-items: center`) centers on both axes without knowing the box's dimensions. The classic gotchas: `margin: 0 auto` only centers horizontally and needs a set width; `text-align: center` only affects *inline* content, not the block itself; `vertical-align: middle` only works on inline/table-cell contexts. Flex/grid superseded all the old translate/absolute hacks.

prompt: `em` is relative to the parent's font size and compounds when nested. What is the computed `font-size` of `.inner`?
explanation: `em` resolves against the *parent's* computed font-size, so it compounds through nesting. `.outer` = 16 x 1.5 = 24px; `.inner` = 24 x 1.5 = 36px. This compounding is the classic `em` trap — `rem` avoids it because it is always relative to the *root* (`<html>`) font size, so `1.5rem` would be 24px at every nesting depth.

prompt: You are writing CSS mobile-first. Which media-query pattern fits that approach?
choices: Write base styles for small screens, then layer enhancements with `@media (min-width: 768px) { ... }` for larger screens
Write base styles for desktop, then override with `@media (min-width: 768px)` for phones
Use only `@media (max-width: 768px)` and never write base styles
Mobile-first means avoiding media queries entirely
explanation: Mobile-first means the default (unqualified) styles target the smallest screen, and you progressively *add* complexity for wider viewports using `min-width` queries. `min-width: 768px` applies at 768px **and up**; `max-width: 768px` applies at 768px **and down**. Building up with `min-width` keeps the base lightweight and tends to produce simpler, less-overridden CSS than a desktop-first `max-width` cascade.

prompt: What does the selector `h2 + p` match?
choices:
The first `

` that immediately follows an `

` (adjacent-sibling combinator)

Every `

` that appears anywhere after an `

` (that is what `+` means)

Every `

` nested inside an `

`

Any `

` that is a direct child of an `

`
explanation: `+` is the adjacent-sibling combinator: it matches the element that is the very next sibling. Contrast the family: `>` = direct child (`ul > li`), `~` = *any* later sibling (general sibling), a space = any descendant at any depth, `:nth-child(2n)` = structural position (even children), and `::before`/`::after` = generated pseudo-elements (two colons) versus state pseudo-classes like `:hover` (one colon).

prompt:
A teammate builds a clickable control as `
Save
` and asks whether to add `role="button"` to make it accessible. Best guidance?
choices: Use a native `<button>` instead — the first rule of ARIA is to prefer a native element, which gives focusability, Enter/Space activation, and the button role for free
Yes — adding `role="button"` alone fully makes the div accessible
Add `role="button"` and `aria-label` and it will be keyboard-operable automatically
Divs can never be made accessible, so the click handler should be removed
explanation: The first rule of ARIA: don't use ARIA if a native element already conveys the semantics and behavior. A `<button>` is focusable, fires on Enter/Space, and is announced as a button — none of which a `<div onclick>` does. Retrofitting the div correctly would require `role="button"` **plus** `tabindex="0"` **plus** manual key handlers for Enter/Space — reinventing what `<button>` gives you for free, and easy to get wrong.

prompt: Walk through how you would make a *custom* component accessible — either a custom dropdown/select (listbox) or a modal dialog. What must you handle beyond the visuals?

reveal.answer: Accessibility for a custom widget means replicating the semantics, keyboard behavior, and focus handling that a native control would provide. You expose roles/state via ARIA, drive it entirely from the keyboard, and manage focus deliberately — because a purely mouse/visual implementation is invisible and unusable to screen-reader and keyboard users.
points: Native first: if a plain `<select>`, `<details>`, or `<dialog>` works, use it and skip most of this. Only hand-roll when design truly requires it.

Roles & state (dropdown): trigger button with `aria-haspopup="listbox"` and `aria-expanded`; the popup as `role="listbox"` with `role="option"` children and `aria-selected` on the active option; link them with `aria-activedescendant` or roving `tabindex`.
Roles & state (modal): `role="dialog"` + `aria-modal="true"`, labelled by its heading via `aria-labelledby` (and `aria-describedby` for body text).
Keyboard operability: open/close and choose with Enter/Space; Arrow keys move through options; Home/End jump; type-ahead for lists; **Escape** closes. Every mouse action needs a key equivalent.
Focus management: move focus into the dialog/list on open, **trap** focus inside (Tab/Shift+Tab cycle within, don't leak to the page behind), and **return focus** to the trigger on close.
Inert background: hide the rest of the page from AT while a modal is open (`inert` attribute or `aria-hidden` on siblings) so screen readers can't wander behind it.
Visible focus indicator: never remove focus outlines without replacing them — keyboard users must see where they are (`:focus-visible`).
Announcements: use an `aria-live` region for dynamic status (e.g. 'no results', 'item added') so changes are spoken without moving focus.
Also: sufficient color contrast (WCAG AA ~4.5:1 for text), meaningful `alt` text on any image content, and don't convey state by color alone.
reveal.trap: A weak answer stops at ARIA roles and thinks sprinkling `aria-*` attributes is enough. ARIA changes what's *announced* but adds **zero** behavior — without keyboard handlers, focus trapping, focus return, and Escape-to-close, the widget is still broken for real users. ARIA is a promise you must implement, not a fix.
